caveats: a: BLS occupation shares are May 2023 and do not report task time or current automation within occupations.
a: The laboratory survey has 110 self-selected respondents, is Europe-skewed, favors larger labs, and is concentrated in food and beverage.
a: The frozen compensation intensity combines 2024 wage data with 2022 receipts and a harmonized benefits adjustment; that vintage mismatch is separate from this task-exposure estimate.
rho: Neither survey measures implemented labor substitution at the screened firms.
rho: Biopharma quality-control laboratories face stricter validation than some NAICS 541380 segments and are often captive rather than outsourced.
rho: EPA, FDA, and NIST requirements demonstrate constraints in regulated or accredited niches but do not quantify their prevalence across the industry.
e: No source reports the eligible share of firms in the specified earnings band.
e: The NAICS code spans laboratory and on-site testing modalities with different repeat-revenue and transferability profiles.
e: The frozen firm count is an estimate bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% margin assumption, so actual band membership may differ materially.
s5: Gallup measures plans rather than completed transactions and covers all small employer businesses.
s5: EY and SGS evidence is global and includes larger testing, inspection, certification, and assurance businesses outside the lens.
s5: Public deal announcements do not reveal the full denominator or failed transactions.
q: No source directly measures five-year retained AI benefit for lower-middle-market testing firms.
q: Oregon State's fee schedule demonstrates billing architecture at a public laboratory, not private-market competitive behavior.
q: Intertek is a global scaled operator with a broader assurance, testing, inspection, and certification mix.
d5: BLS does not publish six-digit real output for this industry in the cited table.
d5: Employment is affected by productivity and therefore is not a direct measure of service quantity.
d5: The EY forecast is global and spans inspection and certification as well as laboratory testing.
o: The cited programs are compliance-heavy examples rather than an industry-wide demand census.
o: Accreditation can be voluntary or customer-required, and not every service in the NAICS code is regulated.
o: Sensor-based monitoring and customer laboratory investment could reduce outsourced test volume more quickly than assumed.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External commercial, industrial, and public-sector customers buy tests through per-sample or per-test fees, project scopes, recurring monitoring programs, master service agreements, and related reporting or certification work.
lens.excluded_activities: Medical and veterinary laboratories, captive internal quality-control units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without a recurring or repeat outsourced-service model.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market laboratories and field-testing firms that repeatedly provide physical, chemical, biological, calibration, electrical, geotechnical, mechanical, nondestructive, thermal, or related analytical testing to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages by occupation in NAICS 541380, while O*NET describes tasks for the industry's largest production occupation and the laboratory survey measures current process digitization in a sample concentrated in European QA/QC laboratories. I mapped reporting, data transfer, sample registration, document review, scheduling, analysis support, and administrative work to AI-exposed tasks, while treating sample handling, instrument setup, calibration, observation, and physical tests as largely unexposed, then bounded a wage-weighted industry share. The laboratory sample's larger facilities and the inclusion of internal QA/QC labs may overstate digital task exposure; omission of task-level time shares and AI assistance within scientific occupations may understate it.
caveats: BLS occupation shares are May 2023 and do not report task time or current automation within occupations.
The laboratory survey has 110 self-selected respondents, is Europe-skewed, favors larger labs, and is concentrated in food and beverage.
The frozen compensation intensity combines 2024 wage data with 2022 receipts and a harmonized benefits adjustment; that vintage mismatch is separate from this task-exposure estimate.
a.change_evidence: A representative time-and-motion study across independent US testing laboratories, with hours and fully loaded pay assigned to sample intake, instrument work, review, reporting, sales, administration, and rework, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is anchored by hands-on science, engineering, inspection, and instrument work, but also contains substantial management, office, business, computer, analysis, documentation, and report-production labor. BLS reports that life/physical/social science occupations are 24.44% of employment, architecture/engineering 16.27%, production 13.64%, office/administrative support 11.90%, management 10.83%, business/financial 6.95%, and computer/mathematical 2.78%. O*NET shows the core inspection role combining physical testing and calibration with data recording, analysis, and written reports. The resulting exposure is meaningful but well below a software-only workflow.

d5.bridge: BLS projects US six-digit industry employment, not constant-price demand, and publishes real output only for the broader architectural, engineering, and related-services parent. EY forecasts nominal or unspecified-price global testing, inspection, and certification market growth, which is broader geographically and by service. I interpret six-digit employment growth as a conservative activity floor, use the parent's real-output trend as a productivity-adjusted anchor, and haircut the global market forecast for inflation and scope to obtain a five-year constant-price, constant-quality ratio. Parent-sector breadth and global regulation may bias growth upward, while employment can understate demand when laboratory productivity rises.
caveats: BLS does not publish six-digit real output for this industry in the cited table.
Employment is affected by productivity and therefore is not a direct measure of service quantity.
The EY forecast is global and spans inspection and certification as well as laboratory testing.
d5.change_evidence: BEA or Census five-year constant-price revenue indexes at NAICS 541380, separated into price, test volume, mix, and quality, plus customer-industry forecasts by test category, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: BLS projects testing-laboratory employment rising 3.9% from 2024 to 2034, while its broader parent industry's real output grows 1.7% annually. EY expects global testing, inspection, and certification growth of 4% to 5% annually, citing regulation and emerging digital technologies. Together these support modest real growth over five years, with a downside near flat if industrial volumes weaken and an upside from new standards, materials, electronics, environmental monitoring, and outsourced compliance testing.

caveats: No source reports the eligible share of firms in the specified earnings band.
The NAICS code spans laboratory and on-site testing modalities with different repeat-revenue and transferability profiles.
The frozen firm count is an estimate bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% margin assumption, so actual band membership may differ materially.
e.change_evidence: A business-level census of US firms in the screened earnings band, coding external revenue share, repeat-customer revenue, accreditation transferability, customer concentration, owner dependence, and captive status, would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is predominantly an outsourced analytical-service model, so most lower-middle-market firms should meet the external-customer and repeat-service lens. The haircut removes project-only operations with little repeat business, captive or affiliated units, businesses whose accreditation or permits cannot transfer, firms dependent on nontransferable owner expertise, and operations with unacceptable customer concentration or quality liabilities.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541380 ($17,068,820,469) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541380 ($26,656,582,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $172,295 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541380); class <5: 2,444 firms, avg revenue/firm $373,954 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $58,524 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 961 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,076,685 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $168,501 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 734 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,220,721 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $347,543 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 749 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,303,646 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $986,521 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 187 firms, avg revenue/firm $26,036,572 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,074,724 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 151 firms, avg revenue/firm $89,322,291 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,978,939 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 187

o.bridge: EPA drinking-water certification, FDA ASCA participation, and NIST accreditation describe operator requirements in specific regulated or accredited testing programs, not the share of all NAICS 541380 demand requiring a testing-firm operator. I extrapolate from their requirements for approved physical methods, competent personnel, proficiency testing, equipment control, onsite assessment, traceability, and accountable reports, then haircut for voluntary tests that customers may insource or replace with embedded sensors and software. Regulated examples may bias operator need upward across the whole code; continued customer demand for independent evidence may make the haircut too large.
caveats: The cited programs are compliance-heavy examples rather than an industry-wide demand census.
Accreditation can be voluntary or customer-required, and not every service in the NAICS code is regulated.
Sensor-based monitoring and customer laboratory investment could reduce outsourced test volume more quickly than assumed.
o.change_evidence: A representative customer survey and lost-revenue analysis that separates tests eliminated, insourced, converted to continuous sensors, supplied by equipment vendors, and retained with independent laboratories would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Most current services culminate in a defensible result produced from a physical sample, item, site, or calibrated instrument. EPA certification requires approved methods, annual proficiency testing, and audits; FDA's ASCA framework requires accredited laboratories and test reports; NIST accreditation evaluates personnel, methods, equipment, traceability, sampling, handling, and reporting. Software can reduce effort and some customers can self-test, but most year-five quantity still needs an accountable laboratory or field-testing operator.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year retained AI benefit for lower-middle-market testing firms.
Oregon State's fee schedule demonstrates billing architecture at a public laboratory, not private-market competitive behavior.
Intertek is a global scaled operator with a broader assurance, testing, inspection, and certification mix.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level data from independent US laboratories showing billing basis, renewal frequency, realized price changes, bid win/loss rates, customer concentration, and gross-margin change after workflow automation would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Per-test, per-sample, setup, rush, pickup, shipping, and contracted-service charges decouple customer prices from the seller's internal labor hours, so a laboratory can retain a majority of implemented productivity benefit before renewal repricing. Intertek attributes margin progress partly to pricing, productivity, mix, and operating leverage, showing that a large operator can hold some efficiency gains. Retention is reduced by competitive bidding, procurement scrutiny, customer sharing at renewal, required reinvestment in quality systems and instruments, and volume-based price concessions.

rho.bridge: The 1LIMS survey measures digital maturity and integration in 110 mostly European, generally large QA/QC laboratories, and Deloitte measures modernization plans among 103 biopharma executives rather than implementation of AI-exposed tasks at US lower-middle-market independent laboratories. I use current integration gaps as a lower-friction signal and stated two-to-three-year automation plans as an adoption signal, then haircut for five-year workflow redesign, validation, staff adoption, instrument integration, and accreditation constraints. Large respondents and vendor interest likely bias implementation upward; slower legacy integration and conservative change control could make even the low case optimistic.
caveats: Neither survey measures implemented labor substitution at the screened firms.
Biopharma quality-control laboratories face stricter validation than some NAICS 541380 segments and are often captive rather than outsourced.
EPA, FDA, and NIST requirements demonstrate constraints in regulated or accredited niches but do not quantify their prevalence across the industry.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of US independent laboratories with baseline workflow maps and five-year realized automation hours, segmented by LIMS maturity, accreditation regime, instrument age, and laboratory size, would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Digital foundations exist but are incomplete: 95% of surveyed labs use digital tools, yet only 11% report fully connected instruments and software, 21% are fully digital, and two-thirds of LIMS users have integration gaps. Deloitte similarly finds 40% reporting disconnected systems or limited automation, even as 56% expect more automated or predictive operations within two to three years. AI can implement much of the exposed documentation and data work over five years, but regulated methods, audit trails, validation, exception handling, and human signoff prevent full conversion.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports succession intentions for US small employer-business owners, not completed qualifying transfers among eligible lower-middle-market testing firms. I start near the 22% five-year sell-or-transfer intention for employer owners and allow a modest industry uplift because EY documents sustained testing, inspection, and certification acquisition activity and SGS documents repeated US laboratory bolt-ons. Intentions can fail or become non-control/internal transfers, biasing the estimate upward; larger screened firms and an active strategic-buyer channel may bias the all-small-business benchmark downward.
caveats: Gallup measures plans rather than completed transactions and covers all small employer businesses.
EY and SGS evidence is global and includes larger testing, inspection, certification, and assurance businesses outside the lens.
Public deal announcements do not reveal the full denominator or failed transactions.
s5.change_evidence: A transaction database matched to a complete denominator of eligible US testing firms, with control status, deal completion, internal reorganizations, and failed sale processes identified, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Gallup finds that 22% of employer-business owners intend to sell or transfer their business within five years and that 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people aged 55 or older. Industry deal flow is visibly active: EY counts almost 1,000 global testing, inspection, and certification acquisitions over twelve years, while SGS reports 24 bolt-ons across 2024-2025 and named US laboratory acquisitions. Those facts support a meaningful but minority five-year control-transfer probability.

principal_driver: A large repeatable documentation and data layer sits around physical, often accredited testing that customers still need from an accountable operator.
principal_weakness: Instrument integration, validation, and quality-control constraints may keep exposed labor from becoming implemented savings, while procurement can reclaim part of any benefit.

caveats: S1 labels the 13.64% share as production employment, but that figure is Other Production Occupations; total Production Occupations are 15.85%. The correction modestly increases the visibly hands-on occupation share but is not large enough by itself to change the base tier or action.

The a bridge does not expose occupation-level task allocations or task-time weights. Its broad range and PROXY label are honest, but the 0.38 base remains a judgment layered on supported occupation and task descriptions.

The rho evidence comes from mostly European, larger QA/QC laboratories and biopharma executives rather than US lower-middle-market independent laboratories. The direction of bias is discussed, but realized five-year implementation remains weakly anchored.

The q sources establish fee architecture and mixed large-operator margin drivers, not the fraction of AI productivity benefit retained by target firms. The ESTIMATE label is appropriate, but placing the entire q range above the factor cap suppresses visible C-factor sensitivity despite the absence of direct retention evidence.

The s5 bridge relies on stated five-year intentions and global strategic-buyer activity without a denominator of eligible US testing firms or completed qualifying control transfers. SGS's 24-acquisition period is also misstated as 2024-2025 rather than January 2025-February 2026.

The d5 base combines six-digit employment, broader-parent real output, and global TIC nominal-or-unspecified-price growth. This is a disclosed proxy, but it cannot distinguish testing volume, price, quality, and mix for US 541380 firms.

The o range is inferred from three compliance-heavy accreditation programs rather than an industry-wide demand census. The physical-service thesis is credible, but the narrow 0.82-0.96 range understates variation across laboratory and field-testing modalities.

The code spans materially different laboratory and on-site testing modalities even though heterogeneous is set to false. The common physical-testing lens is coherent and disclosed, but segment-level implementation, transferability, and demand durability may differ substantially.

sources_audited.note: The May 2023 NAICS 541380 occupation table supports the cited management, business/financial, computer/mathematical, architecture/engineering, life/physical/social science, and office/administrative shares. The stated 13.64% is the share for Other Production Occupations, while total Production Occupations are 15.85%.
O*NET supports the mixed task description: physical inspection, measurement, sampling, instrument adjustment and calibration coexist with data recording, analysis, and report writing.

The page supports the 110-response survey, 95% digital-tool use, 11% full connectivity, 21% fully digital status, two-in-three LIMS integration gaps, and the disclosed European, large-lab, food-and-beverage, self-selection, and vendor limitations.

Deloitte supports the 103-biopharma-executive population, 40% digitally siloed or limited-automation finding, and 56% expectation of more automated and predictive capability within two to three years.

The 2022 NAICS manual defines 541380 as establishments performing physical, chemical, and other analytical testing, including the listed biological, calibration, electrical/electronic, geotechnical, mechanical, nondestructive, and thermal tests in laboratories or on-site, and excludes medical and veterinary testing.
The BLS table supports 2024-2034 employment growth of 3.9% for 541380 and 1.7% annual real-output growth for the broader 541300 parent; it provides no six-digit output series, as disclosed.

Intertek attributes 2025 margin progression to a mixture of pricing, productivity, portfolio mix, operating leverage, and cost discipline. It supports the existence of mixed efficiency and pricing contributions, but does not isolate retained productivity benefit or support the q value.
EY supports expected global TIC growth of 4-5% annually, digital and regulatory drivers, and almost 1,000 global acquisitions over twelve years. Its broader global TIC scope is accurately disclosed.

SGS supports 24 bolt-on acquisitions and names relevant US laboratory, testing, calibration, and forensics transactions. The packet's 'across 2024-2025' wording is inaccurate: the release describes acquisitions from January 2025 through February 2026.

Gallup supports the 52.3% age statistic and the 22% five-year sell-or-transfer intention among employer-business owners, while clearly measuring plans rather than completed qualifying control transfers.
EPA supports certification, approved methods, annual proficiency testing, periodic on-site audits, and integrity procedures for drinking-water compliance laboratories.

FDA supports ISO/IEC 17025-based ASCA accreditation, test-plan development, complete and summary test reports, competence information, and scope controls for participating medical-device testing laboratories.

NIST supports technical-competence evaluation, on-site assessment, proficiency testing, and requirements covering personnel, methods, equipment, traceability, sampling, item handling, and reports under NVLAP.
Oregon State's July 2025-June 2026 schedule supports per-sample and per-analysis fees plus setup, hourly laboratory service, shipping, and pickup charges.
